results.description:
A small fragment from the socket of a Late Bronze Age socketed axehead, dating c. 1150 to  750 cal BC.

The socket appears to have been rectangular in plan originally with a double collar, the lower of which is a shallow raised line, the upper is far thicker. A casting line is present on the centre of the inside surface. Both surface are a mottled dark green colour.

The fragment is 33.6mm long, 23.2mm wide, 6.1mm thick and weighs 11.67 grams.

The square socket and double collar are indicative that this dervies from a socketed axehead see for example OXON-F166E7. The double collar might indicate it comes from a South Eastern type although too little survives to be certain.

An incomplete copper-alloy socketed axehead of Late Bronze Age date, Ewart Park Phase c.1000 - 800 BC.

The axe head is broadly rectangular with a square socket mouth of uneven thickness, just over half of which is lost to worn breaks. A raised collar is present around what remains of the mouth and a second collar is present below.

Internally the socket tapers and narrows from the mouth to the cutting edge. Externally the axehead has broadly parallel sides before flaring slightly into the blade and cutting edge. a small sub-rectangular portion of one side of the axehead is lost to worn breaks.

An integral D-sectioned loop sits to one side of the socket, extending from the lower collar into the body of the axehead. A casting seam is visible externally running longitudinally down both sides of the axe head and the loop. The object is undecorated.

The metal has a mid-green delaminated patina and is worn. The axehead is 101.89mm long, 38.7mm wide at the blade and 38.7mm wide at the socket, the walls are 5mm thick at the socket mouth. The axehead weighs 172g.

Two separate portions of the axehead, and smaller fragments from the body are also present though broken from the main object.

Socket fragment 1: 29.7mm x 24.4mm x 4.8mm; 12.1g.

Socket fragment 2: 20.4mm x 16.4mm x 4.2mm; 4.8g.

Body fragments: 1.7g.

The object can be classified as the Ewart park phase of metal work which corresponds with metalworking phases X - XII, or Needham Periods 6-7, dating from about 1150-750 CAL BC. This axe is likely to date from about 1000 - 800 BC.

The fragments were all found within 23 meters.

A complete copper alloy palstave of Middle to Late Bronze Age date (1250-950 BC). The cutting edge has flared edges which taper in toward the handle end. The cutting edge is crescentic and slightly flared. There is a stop-ridge halfway down the length of the palstave. The loop is integral and is situated on the surface between the two recessed portions. The flanges and septum form an H shape. The casting lines on the body are prominent at the butt end, but ground along the blade end.

Length: 141.81mm, Width: 45.29mm, Thickness: 33.98mm, Weight: 402.80g

The metal is a dark green in colour with a slightly pitted patina.

This example shares the strongest similarities with examples which fall into the transitional / late forms, dating to the Penard or early Wilburton metalwork phases. An example with a similar form has been recorded from South Oxfordshire: OXON-452316, Hampshire: SUR-F560A3 and Shropshire: HESH-D1216F. The recorder notes that similar plain examples (without mid ribs on the blade) are recorded in Schmidt and Burgess (1981), Plate 64, Nos. 880-881, which are grouped into the transitional forms.

A fragment of a Bronze Age palstave, circa 1250-750BC. Only a small part of the butt survives. The fragment has flanges above and below the main body with little apparent surface loss. The breaks are old and patinated. Two of the breaks run across an oval-shaped void which might have been an air bubble within the original casting, which made this a particularly weak point. The object has a mid-green patina. The butt is slightly pitted whereas the main body is smooth on both sides.

Length 22mm, height 24mm, width 12mm. Weight 17.04g.

An incomplete flint-tempered late Bronze Age to early Iron Age handmade coarseware bowl or jar of the Post-Deverel-Rimbury 'Plainware' group dating prior to 800 BC. The vessel retains half of its flat base and sides to a surviving height of around 98.5mm. The walls are around 10mm thick and the base has a diameter of 84mm. The fabric is a reduced dark grey-brown and tempered with moderate (10%) poorly sorted subangular calcined grey flint (up to 3mm). There is no decoration.

A complete, copper alloy, transitional Palstave dating from the Middle to Late Bronze Age, Penard or early Wilburton metalwork phases, c. 1250-950 BC.

The palstave is complete and remains in good condition. It is single looped and has a near straight, squared stop ridge. The butt is slightly slanted and has a rectangular cross section. The septum gradually widens in thickness towards the stop ridge, the flanges begin roughly halfway along it, extending outward as they meet the stop ridge. The blade itself has a convex, smooth surface with no ribs visible. It gradually tapers in thickness from the stop ridge until the last quarter, where it adopts a triangular cross section as it narrows towards the cutting edge. The edges of the blade flare outwards producing a curved edge which shows minimal signs of use. The side of the palstave without the loop has a casting seem visible running down the centre. The loop itself begins after the stop ridge. The copper alloy surface is in a good state of preservation and is a dark brown colour, with sandy coloured patches of encrusted soil and red / orange patches, possibly the result of ferrous elements in the soil.

The palstave is 158.5mm long. The length of the blade is 87.4mm, the stop ridge and septum are 75mm. The butt is 23.3mm in width, the stop ridge 30.2mm, the blade 26.5mm (after the stop ridge) and 44.5mm (at the cutting edge). The butt is 6.6mm thick, the stop ridge 32.1mm, the blade 20.3mm (after the stop ridge) and 2.6mm (at the cutting edge). The loop is 21.5mm long, 11.9mm wide and 8.7mm thick. The palstave weighs 42.7g.

This palstave shares the strongest similarities with examples which fall into the transitional / late forms, dating to the Penard or early Wilburton metalwork phases. An example with a similar form has been recorded from South Oxfordshire: OXON-452316. The recorder notes that similar plain examples (without mid ribs on the blade) are recorded in Schmidt and Burgess (1981), Plate 64, Nos. 880-881, which are grouped into the transitional forms. Other comparable examples recorded on the PAS database include SUR-F560A3, BH-DDEA57, HAMP-CEA8F1 and WILT-B80953.

A complete copper-alloy socketed gouge of late Bronze Age date, c.1000 - 800 BC. Class I. Wilberton, Ewart Park or Llyn Fawr metalworking phases.

The object comprises a hollow circular socket which extends into a U-shaped blade terminal to one end. From the socket end the sides tapers to a rounded cutting/gouging edge which is concave to one side. The gouging edge is worn and the object undecorated. The socket is filled with organic material from its deposition.

The gouge is 92.4mm long, and 16mm x 6.2mm at the cutting edge. The socket is circular with an exterior diameter of 20.5m. The object weighs 74.9g.

Comparable socketed gouges have been recorded on the PAS database. For examples see: YORYM-316F81, YORYM-CD7FFB, SWYOR-FCB525, NMGW-62C84F, DUR-06F0E8, IOW-EC25A7 and SUR-7898C5. The latter record notes that: "Socketed gouges have been found associated with metalwork assemblages of Wilberton (eg. Guilsfield, Savory, 1980, No. 268. 64-65) through Ewart Park (eg Penwyllt, Savory 1980, No 284. 7-8) and Llyn Fawr (Savory 1980, No 291 9-11). Savory (ibid., p 55) suggests that the earliest examples have multiple mouth mouldings, while later examples have a plain or broad, flat collar. According to Pearce in the 1983 publication 'The Bronze Age Metalwork of South Western Britain Part I', page 43, socketed gouges 'appear to have begun in the Wilburton phase, and continued as part of the tool repertoire until the end of the Bronze Age', although she suggests that most socketed gouges can be dated to the Ewart phase between c. 1000 - c. 800 BC. Needham et al's (1997) recent study of Bronze Age chronology dated a collared socketed gouge from the Bodwrog Hoard, Anglesey to 2720 +- 45 BP (DoB No 3, OxA-4652, p68)."

Ref:


	Knight, M., Ormrod, T. and Pearce, S., (2015). The Bronze Age Metalworking of South Western Britain: A corpus of material found between 1983 and 2014. Oxford: Archaeopress.
	Needham, S.P., (1996). Chronology and periodisation in the British Bronze Age. Copenhagen: Wiley.
	Needham, S.P., Bronk Ramsay, C., Coombs, D., Cartwright, C., & Petitt, P., (1997). An independent chronology for British Bronze Age metalwork: the results of the Oxford Radiocarbon Accelerator Programme. Archaeological Journal 179:1, pp. 1-31.
	Pearce, S.M., (1983). The Bronze Age Metalwork of South Western Britain. Oxford: British Archaeological Reports.
	Savory, H.N., (1980). Guide Catalogue of the Bronze Age Collections. Cardiff: National Museums and Galleries of Wales.
results.subClassification: Wilberton, Ewart Park or Llyn Fawr metalworking phase

results.description: A complete cast copper alloy arrowhead of Middle to Late Bronze Age date (c. 1300-800 BC), 57mm in length. The arrowhead is narrow and triangular, with a lenticular cross section which has a slightly rounded midrib. The rear end has two small and slightly rearward pointing triangular barbs flanking a flat rectangular tang.
results.notes:
Bronze Age copper-alloy barbed and tanged arrowheads are known from Britain, though they are rare finds -  this only the third example recorded from Hampshire. Other examples on the database include SWYOR-3A2CF7, NMS-B061B6, NMS-F982A3, WMID-CE6A76, NARC-F9B223, NMS-477E93 and SF6680. In contrast there is only one example known from a secure British Bronze Age archaeological context (the Penard hoard, Archaeologica 71, page 138). At the time of its discovery it was a unique find in Britain and was presumed to be an import on the basis of a range of previous finds of copper-alloy arrowheads recorded from Northern France. The PAS recorded finds have since demonstrated that these copper-alloy arrowheads were also a British tradition. Although the barbed and tanged form was used in flint from the Early Bronze Age, the Penard find was dated to 1275-1140 BC, suggesting at least a Middle Bronze Age date for copper alloy examples such as this one.

Recorded from details emailed by the finder.

results.description:
An incomplete copper alloy middle to late Bronze Age tanged knife or dagger, probably dating to  c. 1150-850 BC. The object consists of part of the tang, and the blade. The blade is triangular in shape, with a rounded tip. There is a raised ridge running down the centre of the blade, stopping at the tang, making the blade a flattened lozenge shape in cross section. The tang projects from the wider edge of the blade, it is narrower than the blade, with a worn break along the outer edge. There is part of a circular perforation still visible along the broken edge. The blade is worn, with notches along the edges. The object has a mid brown patina, and measures 86.7mm in length, 19.9mm in width, 4.1mm in thickness, and weighs 19.68g.

The knife is similar to SF-E21E0E, DOR-78C28A , OXON-B6BCF8,  BUC-7FF6A7 and  BUC-768275.
A complete cast copper-alloy awl 32.8mm in length. Both ends taper from a thickened mid point (which is offset close to one end) to rounded chisel tips. There is no decoration.
results.notes:
Plain copper alloy awls are hard to date precisely but examples of this form are thought to have been introduced in the Bronze Age (2150-800 BC) with most being Late Bronze Age in date (1150-800 BC).

Recorded from details emailed by the finder.

results.description:
An incomplete copper-alloy late Bronze Age socketed axehead. Sub-rectangular in a cross-section with a socketed mouth, crescentic cutting blade and a single side loop (now missing). The axe has a double mouth moulding, the upper of which is more prominent. A section is missing to one side of the socket leaving an irregular, jagged break. A casting seam can be seen running central down each side. To one side a loop would have projected just below the break, but this is also now missing and only a small stub representing its broken inner end remains. The faces are plain. The sides curve outwards abruptly behind the blade end. The cutting edge is abraded and chipped in places, with its outer corners missing. The surfaces are pitted with corrosion and damaged in places.

This is an example of a south-eastern Type socketed axe belonging to Needham's (1990) Type A1 Plain. Needham (ibid. 30) writes that these are almost exclusively found in Ewart Park/Carps Tongue contexts in Britain (c.1100-800 BC).
results.classification: South-eastern Type; Needham's (1990) Type A1 Plain

results.description: A complete cast copper-alloy awl 58.6mm in length. One half has a rounded cross section and tapers to a point, the other has a rectangular cross section and tapers to a rounded chisel tip.
results.notes: Plain copper alloy awls are hard to date precisely but examples of this form are thought to have been introduced in the Bronze Age (2150-800 BC) with most being Late Bronze Age in date (1150-800 BC).

results.description: A complete cast copper alloy late Middle Bronze Age side looped palstave, 124.2mm in length, of a transitional or late type probably dating to the Penard or early Wilburton metalworking phases (1250-950 cal. BC). There is a single side loop at the mid point on one side. The blade flares out to a rounded cutting edge (36.4mm wide) and has a slightly lenticular cross section. The butt is flat in the centre, 23.5mm wide, with an H shaped cross section; both sides having flanges which start at the butt and expand to join the stop ridge, which is straight and placed at the mid point of the axehead. These stop ridges are pronounced (30.6mm in total depth), lending the side profile a lozengiform shape. There are casting seams running along both sides which have have been filed down. There is no decoration.

An incomplete Middle Bronze Age copper alloy barbed and tanged arrowhead dating to 1300-1100 BC. It is roughly triangular in plan with two barbs, one of which is incomplete, and a sub- rectangular tang as it is broken on one corner. The tang is rectangular in cross section whilst the main body of the arrowhead is a flat lozenge shape, with a low midrib running down its middle from the point to the tang. 

Length: 31.4mm; width: 17.3mm; thickness: 1.9mm; weight: 2.41g.

Arrowheads are uncommon finds in Britain although there are now a good number recorded on the database including WILT-6612D9, SUSS-ECB6BD, DOR-969968, BERK-138F31, NMS-105598, SWYOR-3A2CF7 and OXON-85C4D6 which notes a known example in a hoard is the Llangrove, Penard hoard (Savory 1980: pl 32, Archaeologica 71, 138), and other tentative references are made to examples from Oundle, Northamptonshire (Watson 1993: 6-7), Wetheringsett, Suffolk (Maraszek 2006: ENG/SU 0.NR) and  Exning Forest, Suffolk (Victoria County History Suffolk: 266, 275, 305).

An example of this type of Bronze Age arrowhead was found at the large conflict site in the the Tollense Valley, north-east Germany. Investigations have provided evidence of a large and violent conflict in the thirteenth century BC, which gives further support of a Middle Bronze age date. This is closest to a Type 2A arrowhead. 'Type 2A arrowheads (with spherical tang) which are frequently found from eastern France and the central Rhineland in the west to Bavaria and Moravia in the east. Although the arrowhead found in the Tollense Valley provides the only example of this type from northern Germany' (Inselmann et al, 2024, 1260).

An incomplete copper alloy knife of probable Late Bronze Age date (c.1100-800 BC).

The object consists of a leaf-shaped blade that is missing its tip. The bade has a shallow lozenge-shape section and is extremely thin towards the cutting edges. At the back of the blade is an incomplete integral tang. The tang is flat and sub-rectangular in form with a slight projection to one side. The object has a smooth, glossy surface and a dark green patina.

Dimensions: Length 74.7mm; width 16.3mm; thickness 2.3mm; weight 10.48g

Similarly formed knives dating to the Late Bronze Age are recorded on the PAS database, see for example SF5067 which comments: "This object has been seen briefly by Stuart Needham (formerly) of the British Museum and identified as a knife/razor, as it is too thin to serve as a leaf-shaped arrowhead. The actual function of such objects is still unclear". For other examples, see also NCL-9299B0 and NCL-F3D0E3

An incomplete copper alloy socketed axehead or chisel, dating to the late Bronze Age (c.1000-700 BC).

The object consists of the lower portion of the axehead, mainly the cutting edge and a small portion of the edges. The cutting edge is curved and slightly convex and expands just beyond the width of the body. It is 41.6mm wide. Above the cutting edge, the sides are straight and terminate in an irregular worn break. The breaks present a hollowed interior. The surface has light brown and green patina.

Dimensions: Length 56.9mm; width 41.6mm; thickness 17mm; weight 65.7g

The small size of the object suggests it may be a chisel instead of an axehead, however its incomplete condition makes this difficult to determine. Similar objects which are also recorded as chisels or possible axeheads include: NMS-8E55C7; SWYOR-AC278F and SUR-23E64F

An incomplete cast copper-alloy socketed axehead fragment, probably a ribbed socketed type, dating to the later Bronze Age (c. 950-750 BC). The surviving parts consists of the blade and part of the broken hollow body. The body is sub-trapezoidal in shape and sub-rectangular in cross-section. A faint longitudinal casting seam is visible on the larger surviving triangular side. The blade is thin and crescentic with rounded corners. From it the body gradually thickens to a rectangular recess 10.7mm deep. The blade edge is worn and damaged, while the breaks on the body are similarly old and worn. The exterior surfaces of the axehead have a dark-green patina. There is wear and abrasion across the entire object.

The axehead measures 29.1mm in length, 45.6mm in maximum width, 13.1mm in body thickness at the break and 1.8mm thick at the blade edge. The entire object weighs 41.86 grams.

Bronze Age tanged chisel of Late Bronze Age date, probably of Wilburton or Ewart Park metalworking industry, corresponding with Needham’s (1996) Periods 6-7, dated to c. 1150 – 750BC

The chisel has a tang (50mm long, 7mm at the widest point) with a rectangular cross section that tapers to a point at the top. On the opposite end the chisel has a wide curved cutting edge (27mm long, 35 mm wide).  Separating the bladed from the tang is a wide rectangular collar (8mm long by 12mm wide). 

The following is copied from record NMGW-E96F08

Tanged and collared chisels typically date from the Late Bronze Age to Earliest Iron Age. An example dating to the Wilburton/Wallington phase is known from Doncaster, Yorkshire (Burgess 1968, 19, fig 7,2; Burgess, Coombs and Davies, 1972, 217), though the many may be dated to the Ewart Park phase (c.950-700 BC) and into the subsequent Llyn Fawr period (c.700-600 BC).

Tanged and collared chisels have a broad distribution across northern France, Britain and Ireland (see Coffyn et al. 1981, 202-203, Carte 7), though there are relatively few examples from western England and Wales. A very similar example with a thick collar and triangular blade can be seen in the large Ewart Park phase Nottingham Hill hoard, Gloucestershire (Gingell 1974, 308, fig 4,22), as well as a single find from Leigh Woods, Bristol (MacGregor 1987, 109, 11.65). Another example was found at Brogyntyn, Shropshire (Savory 1980, No 288.3) associated with a socketed gouge and a fragmentary Hallstatt 'C' sword

A complete gold penannular ring dating to the middle to late Bronze Age (c. 1300-800 BC). The ring is circular in both plan and cross-section, with flat, slightly slanted terminals.

These types of gold or gold-on-base-metal rings and penannular rings are characteristic finds of the middle to late Bronze Age (1300-800 BC). They have been termed 'hair-rings' and 'ring-money' (Taylor 1980; Green 1988; Eogan 1994; 1997; Varndell 2001; Hobbs 2003; Meeks et al 2008; O'Connor et al 2008), but their function remains uncertain. It is perhaps most likely that they were personal adornments, possibly worn on the ears or nose, rather than being hair-ornaments or items of exchange.

Relatively few examples have been dated through associated finds, archaeological investigation, or radiocarbon dating. However, burials in Belgium, north-east France, and the Netherlands, in addition to Scottish and Irish hoard associations, have suggested a date belonging to the Ewart Park phase of the Late Bronze Age, between 1000-800 BC (Eogan 1997). However, in recent years, evidence for earlier development and a longer period of manufacture and circulation has been growing (Raftery 2004, 87-8; Needham 2007, 26-33, Figs 17.3-4). Therefore, on comparative stylistic grounds, this penannular ring may be dated to the middle or late Bronze Age (1300-800 BC). Similar undecorated penannular rings on the PAS database include SUSS-E64547, BM-275770 and HAMP-2419C2.

The weight of this is example indicates that it is solid gold with no core as found in some examples.

Measurements: Diameter 17.93mm, Internal diameter 6.19mm, Width between terminals 2.43mm, Thickness 6.01mm, Weight 16.41g

References:

Eogan, G. 1997. ‘Hair-rings’ and European Late Bronze Age society. Antiquity, 71(272), 308-320.

Green, H. S., 1988. A find of Bronze Age" ring-money" from Graianog, Llanllyfni, Gwynedd. The bulletin of the Board of Celtic Studies 35, 87-91

Gwilt, A. 2015. NMGW-04216C: A BRONZE AGE HOARD Web page available at: https://finds.org.uk/database/artefacts/record/id/727558 [Accessed: 11 Mar 2019 09:12:36]

Hobbs, R. 2003. Treasure: finding our past. London: The British Museum Press.

Meeks, N., Craddock, P., Needham, S. 2008. Bronze Age penannular gold rings from the British Isles: technology and composition. Jewellery Studies 11, 13-30

Raftery, B. 2004. Pit 119: Rathgall, Co. Wicklow. In  Roche, H., Bradley, J., Coles, J., Raftery, B. (eds) 2004. From Megaliths to Metal. Essays in Honour of George Eogan. Oxford:Oxbow Books, 83–91

Taylor, J.J. 1980. Bronze Age Goldwork of the British Isles. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press

Varndell, G. 2001. Ringing the Changes: when terminology matters. Antiquity 75,  515-516
